subject: Advanced C++ Programming
description: Marking guidelines for Advanced C++ coding exercises and assessments, covering correctness, memory safety, modern C++ idioms, and code quality.
importantNote: C++ exercises are assessed on correctness, memory safety, use of modern C++ idioms, exception safety, and adherence to C++ Core Guidelines.

general: Correctness: Does the code compile without warnings and produce the expected output for all test cases?
Memory Safety: No leaks (verified with Valgrind/ASan), no use-after-free, no double-free, no undefined behaviour
Modern Idioms: Prefers RAII, smart pointers, STL algorithms, and move semantics over raw new/delete and C-style code
Exception Safety: Functions provide at least the basic guarantee; critical operations provide the strong guarantee
Const Correctness: Member functions that do not modify state are marked const; getters return const references where appropriate
Code Quality: Clear naming, minimal duplication, appropriate comments, and adherence to C++ Core Guidelines

partialCreditPolicy.description: C++ coding assessment partial credit principles

rules.condition: Code compiles but produces incorrect output for some test cases
rules.marksAwarded: Partial credit based on passing test cases — typically 50-70%

rules.condition: Correct logic but undefined behaviour (e.g., missing noexcept on move)
rules.marksAwarded: Deduct 10-20% — UB is a serious defect in C++

rules.condition: Memory leak detected by Valgrind or ASan
rules.marksAwarded: Deduct 20-30% — resource safety is fundamental in C++

rules.condition: Uses raw new/delete where smart pointers are appropriate
rules.marksAwarded: Deduct 10-15% per instance — modern C++ avoids manual memory management

rules.condition: Correct but misses noexcept on move constructor/assignment
rules.marksAwarded: Deduct 5-10% — noexcept on moves is essential for STL compatibility

rules.condition: Missing const on member functions that do not modify state
rules.marksAwarded: Deduct 5-10% — const correctness is a core C++ contract

rules.condition: Data race detected by ThreadSanitizer in concurrent code
rules.marksAwarded: Deduct 20-30% — thread safety is non-negotiable in concurrent programs

rules.condition: Correct but uses C-style casts instead of static_cast/dynamic_cast
rules.marksAwarded: Deduct 5% — C-style casts bypass type safety

expectations: Correct use of classes, constructors, and destructors
Basic RAII with std::unique_ptr and std::vector
Understands copy vs move semantics at a conceptual level
Uses STL containers and iterators correctly
commonErrors: Missing virtual destructor in base class
Not checking for self-assignment in copy assignment operator
Using raw arrays instead of std::vector or std::array
Using C-style casts instead of static_cast

expectations: Correct Rule of Five implementation with noexcept move operations
Proficient use of templates and type traits
Understands value categories and perfect forwarding
Thread-safe code using RAII locks and condition variables
Uses std::optional, std::variant, and structured bindings
Applies CRTP or PIMPL where appropriate
commonErrors: Move constructor not marked noexcept — prevents STL optimisations
Deadlock from locking mutexes in inconsistent order
Not passing predicate to condition_variable::wait() — spurious wakeup risk
SFINAE errors producing cryptic compiler messages

expectations: Mastery of template metaprogramming and concepts
Correct use of memory_order in lock-free atomic operations
Implements custom allocators or memory pools when appropriate
Profiles and optimises for cache efficiency and vectorisation
Understands ABI stability and PIMPL tradeoffs
commonErrors: Incorrect memory_order leading to data races in lock-free code
Memory ordering too conservative (seq_cst everywhere) causing unnecessary performance loss
Coroutine lifetime errors — frame outlives coroutine handle
Over-engineering with templates when a simple virtual dispatch is clearer

criteria.description: Perfect Rule of Five: all five operations correct, noexcept on moves, copy-and-swap assignment, demonstrated with vector reallocation
All five operations correct but missing noexcept on moves, or copy assignment does not handle self-assignment via swap
Copy and move constructors correct; assignment operator has self-assignment bug or memory leak
Destructor and copy constructor correct; move operations missing or incorrect
Destructor correct; copy constructor has shallow copy — memory leak or double free
Class structure present but significant correctness issues in most operations
No meaningful attempt or code does not compile
Stack<T> complete and correct; static_assert present; sum_stack correctly constrained with enable_if or concept; all edge cases handled
Stack correct and functional; sum_stack works but constraint could be tighter or error message clearer
Stack mostly correct; sum_stack compiles but constraint missing or sum_stack has logic error
Stack implementation has 1-2 significant issues (e.g., missing move push); sum_stack present but incorrect
Basic template structure present but significant functionality missing
Minimal correct elements
No meaningful attempt or does not compile
Thread-safe queue correct; both blocking and non-blocking operations work; no data races (ThreadSanitizer clean); demonstrated with producer-consumer
Blocking operations correct; try_push/try_pop have a minor issue; no data races
push/pop mostly correct but spurious wakeup not guarded (no predicate in wait()); or try_push/try_pop missing
Mutex used correctly but condition variables used improperly or not at all — busy-waiting instead
Mutex present but queue not correctly thread-safe
Minimal attempt — queue without any synchronisation
No meaningful attempt or does not compile
All nodes destroyed (no leak); weak_ptr used correctly for prev; forward and backward traversal correct; cycle avoidance explained in comment
Correct but comment on why weak_ptr is needed is missing or incomplete
shared_ptr used for prev — memory leak; or backward traversal via lock() missing
Basic linked list with smart pointers but cycle not avoided
Raw pointers used; destructors not called
No meaningful attempt
Concept correctly defined; print_all constrained and using std::format; operator<=> = default generates all comparisons; sorting and output correct
All features present but if constexpr check for empty is incorrect, or std::format usage has minor issue
Concept defined; operator<=> present; one of the three features has a significant issue
Two of three features (concept, format, spaceship) correctly implemented
One feature correctly implemented; others have significant issues
Some modern syntax present but major compilation errors
No meaningful attempt
